canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, multi-image reasoning is most commonly defined as the latent ability to integrate information from several images to answer questions or infer relations, requiring cross-image comparison and synthesis. This capability is frequently contrasted with the more common focus on single-image visual understanding. A related, less frequent framing is "multi-image comprehension," which one paper defines as the ability to identify shared or consistent elements across visual inputs. To operationalize and measure this construct, papers use several benchmarks, including Mantis-Eval, MMMU, and BLINK. The construct is studied alongside knowledge-based question answering and in-context and few-shot learning. One paper notes that performing this type of analysis necessitates "extended context capacity" from the model.
papers: CoMemo: LVLMs Need Image Context with Image Memory
EasyRef: Omni-Generalized Group Image Reference for Diffusion Models via Multimodal LLM
Reasoning Limitations of Multimodal Large Language Models. A case study of Bongard Problems
MINT-1T: Scaling Open-Source Multimodal Data by 10x: A Multimodal Dataset with One Trillion Tokens
MaVEn: An Effective Multi-granularity Hybrid Visual Encoding Framework for Multimodal Large Language Model
ReMI: A Dataset for Reasoning with Multiple Images

definitions.text: The latent ability to integrate information across multiple images and answer questions or infer relations that require cross-image comparison and synthesis.
definitions.paper: MaVEn: An Effective Multi-granularity Hybrid Visual Encoding Framework for Multimodal Large Language Model

definitions.text: The latent ability of a multimodal model to understand and integrate visual content across multiple images to identify shared or consistent elements.
